text: This is a video of a laparoscopic central gastrectomy for gastric gist. The case is a forty-one-year-old male who was diagnosed with a large ulcerating mass in the gastric body after a workup for iron deficiency anemia. On examination, his physical exam was unremarkable. A CT and EUS showed an eight centimeter mass in the gastric body.

The surgeon retracts the left lateral segment of the liver using a folding retractor, then mobilizes the gastrocolic ligament and enters the lesser sac with a harmonic scalpel. The large gastric tumor is visible bulging through the mid-body of the stomach as the dissection proceeds distally to clear the posterior attachments.

The surgeon performs a neurovascular sparing dissection along the lesser curvature of the stomach using a harmonic scalpel, creating a window from the lesser curve into the lesser sac to facilitate distal transection.
The posterior view shows the dissection of the lesser curvature from the stomach. The surgeon uses a linear laparoscopic stapler to divide the distal margin of the stomach.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the lesser omentum from the lesser curvature of the stomach, using precise surgical techniques to separate these structures.
The surgeon rotates the stomach counterclockwise, providing clear exposure of the proximal transection margin for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon removes the specimen through a small extension of the midline incision, displaying the proximal and distal margins of the stomach.

The surgeon places a 2-0 silk stay suture to stabilize the gastric remnants in preparation for a stapled anastomosis. A gastrotomy is created in the proximal and distal parts of the gastric remnants, followed by the formation of a stapled anastomosis.
The surgeon closes the gastric defect using a PDS suture after ensuring a large enough anastomosis with two staple fires.

His post-operative course was unremarkable. He was advanced to a clear diet on post-op day two and was discharged to home on post-op day three on a full liquid. Final pathology showed an eight-centimeter spindle cell neoplasm, which was positive for CD-CAD. CD-CAD was positive for CD-CAD, CD-CAD was positive for CD-CAD, and CD-CAD was positive for CD-CAD. This was all consistent with the gastrointestinal stromal tumor.
In summary, laparoscopic central gastrectomy is a feasible operation for gastric neoplasm where radical resection is unwarranted. Thank you.
